question.title: Using same Context Everywhere?
question.body:
I am dynamically creating a lot of views within my application. Each requires context.

Should I just use one instance of the context and then pass them to everyone or should I use different instances.

In other words, which is better, this -

Context context = this;

for (int i = 0; i < 10; i++) {
    SomeView view = new SomeView(context);
}

or this -

for (int i = 0; i < 80; i++) {
    SomeView view = new SomeView(this);
}

Note: Views will stay on the screen for a long time.

answers.body:
If you are working in the same class, you can use directly "this",
In case you need the context of other class in current class use Context c = X.class;
Hope it helps.

Please be advised that there is no real difference between your two variants.

In the second example you do not create another instance of this! All that Java does in the background is to pass a pointer as a reference to this to your constructor. Whereas, in the first example, you pass a reference to this to context passing the same reference to your constructor.

This implies that, if you change something in your context inside a view, the context changes in every other view as well. Remember that thread-safety is not given out-of-the-box, be careful.

You might do a read-up on the inner workings of Java: wikipedia is a great start.
